speaker: interviewer
text: You're describing your memories of this resort, St. Valentin In Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were telling us about an image that you had of people that you saw through the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And they have their blanket in their hands. And the two daughters are opening their blankets, like a picnic sort of. They open. And they make sure-- and this was fascinating because they put it on the grass. And they made sure that everything is straight, the corners, very straight. And then they start to help the mother to-- she should sit on there. And instead of sitting down, she's just falling back, straight. And the next thing I notice, they just close the blanket. And they carry her away.

speaker: interviewer
text: For years in my mind, I said what happened? What happened? How can a live person just-- I knew it's dead. I knew that she was dead. But what do they do with this dead? How come they are just taking her away and not staying there, and I should see what happens next? This was in my mind for a long, long time. This is just an image what came back to me now. What, what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that what I remember, we are rounded up again. And we have to move. We have to move. And my grandmother, we cannot take. My mother is begging the German to let the mother come. No way. She's-- and they said that the sick will follow us where we go. They will bring her after us. But, you know, we don't know if we can trust them. And who knows if they are telling us the truth or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are walking, walking again towards the train. The train, I believe, it's not in St. Valentin. I think it's a little bit further out there. I don't if they had a station there maybe-- because it's also a long walk, a very long walk. To me, it seemed a very long walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see the SS this time, the German with a gun. We were quiet. But I don't know, this German here with a gun, somehow, he, he let my mother stay there. And then my mother, after a little while walking, she ran back to, to this swimming pool place, to her mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then a few minutes later-- not a few minute, because it's a distance-- she comes back. She's out of breath. And she does that a few times. And this German is asking her, what are you doing? So she says, I have a very sick mother there. She can't help herself. And I want here to-- I cannot leave her. I cannot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to do. I have my children here. And I have my sick mother there. What shall I-- I don't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she let-- and it's amazing that he let her run back a few times, back and forth. And at one point, he made the decision for her. And she called-- and it's all in German. He said to you, you are a [GERMAN]. You are a, you are a stupid woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here you have your four children. And then you are running back to an old, sick woman. You are going to stay here with your children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next thing I remember is Gostling. We are in Gostling. This is a camp. This camp was originally a-- how do you call those-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where they sell wood. Oh, a lumber yard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lumber yard. This was a lumber yard. But they had wooden barracks there, one wooden barracks. And they have in this place [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] showers. They have showers. And they have outside houses, rows of outside houses. You see, when we arrive to Gostling, when we were-- the door, on one side of the door, soldiers. They were already captured soldiers. I don't know, they were French, or I don't know, most probably. They weren't Hungarian. They were captured all ready. And they were there. And they were, they were taken out from there. I don't know where they went. And the Jews were put in there. There were bunk beds, both side of the barrack, and long tables, not one, but was the length of the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were you together at this point? Do you have any idea? A lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. But it was full. This barrack was full. This was full. And when we arrived, and on the table, those soldiers-- because they left us some, like nothing, cookies, on the table, a pile of it. And I saw that right away. And I started-- but other children, because a lot of children were there-- they saw it. And I, I dashed to that table. Right away I fell on it. I filled up my hands. But I wasn't quick enough to straighten up and get up from there, so all the other children on me. And the grownups also, some of them were there. And they were all trying to grab. Because we were terrible hungry. I couldn't move. I was completely, like, nailed down there with bodies. And the breathing was very hard, I remember. I could hardly breath. I was concentrating that my nose should get a little air.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time everybody got off me-- and the hands, I could feel the hands. Everybody was grabbing, grabbing, grabbing. You could feel nails. I got up. And I ended up with crumbs in my both hands. So my hands were very small. So that was very, very little. I was very disappointed. I was crying, very disappointed-- no food again. So it comes the night, settle on bunks. But the bunks are full of flies and bedbugs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything was just bloody. We were bitten all over.

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger brother, he was, he wasn't so dark skinned as I and very fair skinned. He was blondish. And he, he really got it. He was completely covered with blood, terrible bites. There wasn't a clear spot on his skin. And he got very angry. He stayed in on the bunk bed. And he says, I will show you that I'm going to clean it. I, I'm going to clean up from all the bugs. Of course, he was a child himself. Because he's only two years older than I am. He didn't realize that all the mattresses are fill of those bugs. It is impossible. It's an impossible job. But lot of people who could manage on the table, went on the table and were sleeping there. And we were telling him, come. Come on the table. No, he says. I am going to clean it. You will see. I'm going to clean it. Of course, it was never cleaned. And our body had to get used to it. But were all ready so-- the lice. We had so much lice. So we had to be shaved. We had no hair. We were bald, shaven. My mother, they were given the cutting scissors, shears. And there was no way to keep us clean and, you know, from the lice. At this place, I remember, as they said, they will bring the sick after us on-- there were bundles. And there was a, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a wagon, bundles on the wagon. And on top of the bundles, it's like a pyramid. So I still see it like this. Somebody's yelling, [NON-ENGLISH]. Your mother is there. Your mother is there. And she was there. She was covered. She had-- it was a beautiful scarf, black, sort of very fine material scarf, with red roses and fringes. Beautiful-- it was very pretty. It was very pretty. And that's what she had on. It was hers. She used to have beautiful things. But when she got married and she came to live Hajdunanas when she married my grandfather, she had lovely things. So this was one of her thing. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they brought her to--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took my grandmother down. But that night when-- the day when we arrived, my mother was-- had a friend there from Hajdunana. Most of the people there were Hajdunanas people there and, and the surroundings. And this one, Mrs. Lightner, she had a lot of children, were very poor-- very, very nice, very honest people. And she, she helped my mother, she said, because my mother says, they promised me that my mother will come after me. And I want to make her a-- you know, this bed. It was next to the door. And I want to prepare it for her. She's so sick, so let her have a  little place where she can feel a little comfort.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother, in this Gostling, she was-- there was-- we were surrounded with barbed wire there. And outside of the wire, there was a little house. And there was a person who was watching us. And this happened to be a woman. There were other. There were Germans, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this woman who was-- and she says there is a tavern in [INAUDIBLE], in [INAUDIBLE]-- I don't know if it was Gostling or close by, because they are small places. And they need cleaning if, if you-- and, and my mother says, she says, maybe they will give you something there. So here are there, you can find a person who had a little humanity in them. And my mother grabbed-- she says, oh yes. She will go. She will go. And she says, maybe this friend can come with her. She also-- we were very hungry. The children were very hungry. So they thought that maybe they will give something. And in this tavern, they gave her really a little bit, sugar. And something else they gave there, but just very little. So what she was doing, she was hiding it even from the children, because she was giving-- preparing it for the mother, for my grandmother. I don't know how she could hide it, because we find everything. And my grandmother arrives. And we still had some, some-- I think Mrs. Lightner had a clean sheet. So she gave it. She-- we put it on the bed. My mother and Mrs. Lightner put it on the bed. And when she arrived, they took her off from this wagon, from the pile of bundles. She didn't talk. And my mother says, you know, you are with us. You are going to be with us now. So you will get better. Look, I have some sugar. But she didn't talk. My mother said to us, we should go to sleep. And we listened. And at the end, in the night, I, I woke up. Because I hear my mother is looking for something under the mattress, or somewhere, I don't know. So she says, shh, be quiet. I am just taking it to grandmother, a little bit sugar. I was surprised there is this sugar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened-- the next day, my mother tells me that she talked my grandmother-- because my grandmother, she was just saying something to leave me alone. And my mother says, no, no. Now you are-- you see, I'm going to help you. You will be all right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a little bit of sugar, you will-- I have a little bit of sugar. And you will get back your strength a little bit. Just a-- I just beg of you, take a little teaspoon. And she was going like this. And with her hand, she was saying no. And my mother cried. She says for me, for me, for me, do it. So my grandmother did like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time my mother went back with that little sugar, my mother-- grandmother turned her head to the wall. And she was dead. So what I remember, this part, she died. So I was just sleeping. And I remember that she was looking for the sugar, and going to grandmother, and tell me, just shh, go back to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day, I get up. And somehow the-- I don't see people around me. And every time we had to out from this barrack, we had to pass grandmother, my grandmother, because she was right next to the door, just before you opened the door. I can see it. And I see that she is completely covered with this beautiful shawl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now by this time, I knew that dead is there. I mean, I have seen already a lot. Because I have seen it when we came out from the train and they opened it, that there were-- dead people were taken out. There were a lot of dead people. And when we were coming out from there, and they were giving us a little bit of soup somewhere far, and we heard soup, soup, soup. So I said to my mother-- so my mother put that little-- some dish in my hand, a very tiny, little cup. She say, go and get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked my mother, but how can I go? Look at them. So my mother says, they won't hurt you. I said, yes, but how should I walk? And it didn't come to me that I should walk there. Maybe there is space. Here was the train. So I couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, you just step over it. And make sure you don't step on them. So that's how I walked for the-- [INAUDIBLE] soup, which had to be-- the [INAUDIBLE] has to be pushed away. And that little, called a [INAUDIBLE] [? who ?] [? said ?] nothing. I don't know what was in it. But we swallowed it, because we needed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by this time, I recognized there is an odor of death here. But what happened-- but picture again, it grabs me. And it's in my mind, I can see. I go. And I pass her bed. I see that she's completely covered. I'm scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I see her hand. And it was her right hand. This wasn't some sort of covered. And I said, her hand is cold. She should need-- she needs to be covered, because she's cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go out from the door, from this barrack. And there, I see my mother and my siblings. And they're crying. Everybody's crying. And the friend, and they're standing there, because she was a very known person in Hajdunanas. Everybody knew Mrs. Safar. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said to my mother, you know, grandmother is cold. Her hand is cold. Why didn't you cover her hand? So I attacked her, because I knew that she's dead. I knew that that is terrible, . And I feel awful. And I am crying and sort of yelling at her.

speaker: interviewee
text: You go back and cover her hand, because she's cold. She is cold. My mother says, she's not cold. And I said, yes, she's cold. You go and cover her hand. And my mother says, you go and cover. If you think that she is cold, you go and cover her hand. I said, I cannot cover her hand. You go and cover her hand, because she's cold. And she shouldn't be cold. So my mother says to me, she feels nothing. She's not cold. Listen to me. She's not cold. If you want to cry, cry. But she is not cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the way my grandmother, died, my Safar grandmother died-- terrible, sick, suffering. We don't even know how much she suffered. Because most of the time, she was alone, so sick, and completely helpless-- completely helpless. Was she buried there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she was. This place-- again, this, this German, she said, she says-- they, they, my mother and were asking, begging, let me bury her. They didn't know what to do, first of all, with her. So what happened, she was wrapped up. Well, according to the Jewish law, they prepared her. They washed her. This friend, this Mrs. Lightner, who didn't survive the war, she and my mother, they prepared her, washed her. And according to the Jewish law, they did that. And in this sheet, what was on her mattress, they covered her up. And I remember, and there was a shack there. And this shack was outside of the fence. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- and they were guarding her. According to the Jewish law, you know, they were guarding her, always somebody. My brother also, one of my brothers, that they guarded. And I don't know how long she was at this, she was permitted, and they can bury her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she was wrapped up in this sheet. It seemed to be like a-- so neatly, neatly. The sheet, you could see the head, the shoulder, body, and neatly, it was wrapped up. And, and I want to see her. I just want to go. And I want to see my mother too, because she was always, almost always there. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And they let me. And it was raining. [CRYING] And as it was raining, I go in there. And the rain falls, drip, drip, drip on her, on my grandmother. And I said to my mother, look at her. She's getting wet. Why? She should be pulled away from that. So my mother says, it doesn't make any difference. It doesn't matter to her. She doesn't feel it. And I cry. I said, look, she's getting wet. This cold rain is falling on her. She's cold. She's cold. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: But you see, even in, in there, my mind-- I understood that she doesn't feel. But in other way, I felt that she feels this. She's cold and wet. I feel it. I felt it so much. [CRYING] She is dead. So they bury her, which was permitted to bury her. So she's in Gostling. She's buried there. As I maybe remember, she was-- they let her-- this is a Catholic cemetery-- and they let her against the wall. It was-- it must be there. The priest or somebody there in Gostling, they let her be buried. So for this, we were grateful, grateful for this, that she was buried. We know where she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Gostling, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some people were taken out to the fields to work. Because they were obviously using us for work. And Mrs. Lightner and Mrs. Lightner's daughter, she was a very nice young girl, young lady. She was already tall, neat. And she was working on the field. And she had this-- how do you call it when you cut something, the wheat by hand with a-- Scythe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Scy--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a scythe.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, hold it with a wooden handle--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or a sickle.

speaker: interviewee
text: A sickle, It's a big sickle. That's what she was doing. Hungry, but she was doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family doing at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was cleaning in  the tavern, washing the floor, and whatever they gave her. And, and she was washing the floor in [? hidden-- ?] they were watching it in the little house there outside of the gate every day, washed up the floor. You know, the Germans are very clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but she was grateful to do it, because they gave her a little bit of sugar or a little bit-- something, or you know, a bite of this, a bite of that. And she was very grateful. So she brought it to us. She didn't eat it herself. She gave it to us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long we will in this Gostling. You see, I can't remember the time. Time, I can't remember. I just remembered it's long. And it's suffering. I just-- I only feel-- I only can tell you what I feel. The time to me, it's-- everything is long and takes forever. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this Lightner girl, she comes back one day. And why do I remember her so? Not because I was-- I'm remembering, I just remember people that-- I remember a few children there. Because I was always busy, just my, my little circle. But I remember people there, [? how ?] their moving was there. Why is she's standing out so? Because one day she comes. They brought her in. And she cut her leg very badly with this sickle. And she was really bleeding. And they had to, had to bundle it up. Of course, that was no-- of course, there was no medical attention or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived the war. But she died after the war. And I guess she got married. I don't know after, just from the brother, what she used to tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your family was taken from Gostling?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Gostling, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are taken now to Sankt Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sankt Michael was-- this was really the concentration camp, which was still in the summer time. We were arriving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here, we had-- stayed there for winter. Spring was coming. And this is where we were more stuff of our--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Concentration camp. This was, this was a farm where they were asking people-- they wanted to have people to work for them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was a working farm. And they sent us, my mother and the four children, for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they saw us, when we arrived, they were making fun. This is what they send us? We wanted five people who can work, not children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but then they said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were also walking a long way from the train. Because I don't know if Sankt Michael itself, there is the train or it's a little bit outside. We were walking, walking. And one of the Smith girls, one on the farmer's daughter, came. She had a bicycle. And she walked with us. And she talked with us. And because of Yiddish, we could understand, you know, what she saying-- most of it, not all of it. And she was, you know, all the way, she couldn't stop talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ha, what kind of business is this is that they are sending children when they need people who can work? But children they had to feed and this. And what, what kind of business? He was-- she didn't like it. But she walked us. She walked us to the farm. And-- What, what work did you do there, or did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was on this farm. They were doing-- in-- there was a water flowing there. And they wanted to make a water mill, because they had a mill. And they wanted the water should drive the mill. So this is what's supposed to be built. The people already there, 23 Jews on this farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: 23 Jews on, on a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: On this farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was-- the Smiths were Austrian or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Austrians. This is-- there was-- this is an Austrian family. And they didn't feed us. They didn't give us food, because we had a, a ration, which every week with a wheelbarrow they went-- once my brother went, went to the next little place, [INAUDIBLE]. And then they were rationed out what we get and was brought back. And we had to prepare this ourselves. And I remember that for five of us, we only had just like an ice scoop. You know, let's say they were making a mashed potato, an ice scoop, one ice scoop for mashed potato for five of us. So I always remember. It was very little. We were always hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 23 people were living in, in, in a camp, or in, in a farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 23 people, this was a farm and a big farmhouse. And the farmhouse, as the way you walk in, there's a foyer there. And then you go up on the steps. And there is a landing. And from this landing, there comes a very huge room. And the from this landing comes a smaller room and comes an other room where there is a wheel. And they have already a something, a wheel going there. I don't know what. But it was going. It was all this noise, a wooden thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this one room, this small room, there was a doctor, his wife, and a son, a teenage boy. On the landing, my mother and the four children-- there were two beds. My mother, my sister, and myself, we slept in one bed and my two brothers on the other bed. And from this landing, you could still go higher up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rest of the Jewish people were in this one room. There were all-- you know, beds was there. And there was a stove. And there was a table. And this is where we were staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember some of them, sure. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they? Any from your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: None of them from my-- they were all Hungarians. I think most of them were from-- or some of them were from Szeged. And I don't know from where the rest of it. But they were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were well-to do Jews. They weren't so-- they weren't-- most of them were. And they were educated. One was, as I mentioned, he was a doctor. And this doctor showed to my mother once that he has three little capsules. And he says-- because he was always angry also. He didn't want to talk to anybody. And he says, if they will come and want to harm me, he says, nobody's going to touch me, not myself, not my wife, not my child. They are just going to take this capsule. And we are going to bed and be dead. And nobody's going to touch us. Nobody's going to hurt us. And he was carrying it in the pocket.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he kept to himself. Of course, a doctor, he was a practicing doctor. And he was quite assimilated, you know? Although the rest of the Jewish people there were assimilated Jews. They had mixed marriages. They had, you know, mixed-- So this group of 23 were there for-- from the late summer of '44 through--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were there before we came. I don't know exactly where they-- when they came. But we-- they came then. They were there when we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all were working in some way on that farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? All way-- ?] yes, my sister, they put her into the kitchen there, going to work. She's going to go in the kitchen. I can get her some apples or the pears, because their fruit is up on the mountains. And that's all we needed, mountain air. We were so hungry, and being in this beautiful place. And my brother, my older brother, he was working with Mr. Smith, who was with the ox. And you know how you cut up the earth for planting. I don't how call this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Plow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Plow, yes. You had to hold on the plow. And the oxen, they were pulling them. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you guarded there? Were there SS there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't guarded. They were coming to check on up us. They know that we are there. And the mayor--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll, we'll continue in the next tape, sorry.
